perturbation.original_text: Following the completion of such maintenance term, Bankers Bank may, at its option, renew maintenance for subsequent periods of one (1) year each, subject to adjustments proposed by Netzee not to exceed 5% at least sixty (60) days in advance of the applicable renewal date.
perturbation.changed_text: Following the completion of such maintenance term, Bankers Bank may, at its option, renew maintenance for subsequent periods of one (1) year each, subject to adjustments proposed by Netzee not to exceed 5% at least ten (10) days in advance of the applicable renewal date.
perturbation.explanation: The contract originally requires Netzee to provide notice of adjustments at least 60 days in advance. Reducing this to 10 days may violate reasonable notice periods required under contract law, depending on the jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_citation: UCC § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-309 requires that the time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract, if not otherwise agreed upon, shall be a reasonable time. Reducing the advance notice period from 60 days to 10 days may be deemed unreasonable depending on the complexity and industry standards, potentially leading to a breach of contract claim if the other party is not given sufficient time to evaluate the proposed adjustments.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the advance notice period from 60 to 10 days. The Law Explanation states this timeframe may be deemed unreasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, UCC § 2-309, states that if the time for any action under a contract is not agreed upon, it shall be a reasonable time. Since the contract modification significantly reduces the notice period, it creates an inconsistency that could be deemed unreasonable and therefore contradicts the requirement for a 'reasonable time' under UCC § 2-309(1).

perturbation.original_text: Maintenance provided by Netzee during the term of this Agreement shall include (1) standard maintenance upgrades and modifications offered by Netzee to its customers generally, standard maintenance upgrades and modifications offered by Netzee to its customers generally, and for such purpose Netzee will use commercially reasonable efforts to make and include (without limitation) as such modifications changes and upgrades necessary to comply with generally applicable industry and regulatory requirements of which it is notified (2) 5x12 call-in support between hours of 6:30 a.m. and 6:30 p.m. Eastern Time Monday through Friday, (3) in the event of material discrepancies between the Services as provided and the specifications for such Services (which shall be furnished in reasonably acceptable form), reasonable effort (in relation to importance, impact, and scheduled development plans) to correct those discrepancies, and (4) availability of basic Services with at least 99% uptime between 4:00 a.m Monday and 7:00 p.m. Friday and between 8:00 a.m. Saturday and 2:00 p.m. Saturday Eastern Time, exclusive of outages caused by off-peak prescheduled maintenance or causes beyond Netzee's reasonable control (including third-party equipment or communications).
perturbation.changed_text: Maintenance provided by Netzee during the term of this Agreement shall include (1) standard maintenance upgrades and modifications offered by Netzee to its customers generally, standard maintenance upgrades and modifications offered by Netzee to its customers generally, and for such purpose Netzee will use commercially reasonable efforts to make and include (without limitation) as such modifications changes and upgrades necessary to comply with generally applicable industry and regulatory requirements of which it is notified (2) 5x8 call-in support between hours of 9:00 a.m. and 5:00 p.m. Eastern Time Monday through Friday, (3) in the event of material discrepancies between the Services as provided and the specifications for such Services (which shall be furnished in reasonably acceptable form), reasonable effort (in relation to importance, impact, and scheduled development plans) to correct those discrepancies, and (4) availability of basic Services with at least 90% uptime between 9:00 a.m Monday and 5:00 p.m. Friday and between 10:00 a.m. Saturday and 12:00 p.m. Saturday Eastern Time, exclusive of outages caused by off-peak prescheduled maintenance or causes beyond Netzee's reasonable control (including third-party equipment or communications).
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement specifies 5x12 call-in support and 99% uptime. Reducing this to 5x8 support and 90% uptime might be a breach of contract if the reduced service levels are not commercially reasonable or fail to meet industry standards for banking systems which require high availability. It also means a reduced level of access to banking services during key hours. The change from 99% to 90% uptime is a major change.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Failure to Provide Agreed Upon Services
perturbation.law_explanation: Georgia Code Ann. § 13-4-20 states that a valid contract requires the parties to agree on the same thing in the same sense. By reducing the service levels significantly without proper amendment and agreement, Netzee may be failing to provide the services that Bankers Bank originally contracted for, potentially constituting a breach of contract under Georgia law, especially considering the location of The Bankers Bank as a Georgia banking corporation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 13-4-20 - Requirements as to performance of contractual obligations generally :: 2010 Georgia Code :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Georgia Code 2024 Georgia Code 2023 Georgia Code 2022 Georgia Code 2010 Georgia Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2010 Georgia Code TITLE 13 - CONTRACTS CHAPTER 4 - MODIFICATION, EXTINGUISHMENT, AND RENEWAL ARTICLE 2 - PERFORMANCE § 13-4-20 - Requirements as to performance of contractual obligations generally O.C.G.A. 13-4-20 (2010) 13-4-20. Requirements as to performance of contractual obligations generally Performance, to be effectual, must be accomplished by the party bound to perform, or by his agent where personal skill is not required, or by someone substituted, by consent, in his place, and must be substantially in compliance with the spirit and the letter of the contract and completed within a reasonable time. perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the service levels (call-in support and uptime) significantly. The Law Explanation suggests this might constitute a breach of contract, especially since the parties must agree on the same thing in the same sense (Georgia Code Ann. § 13-4-20). The changed text undermines the original agreement, as the service levels have been reduced without explicit renegotiation that ensures both parties agree to the new terms. While snippet 1 is missing, snippet 2, specifically states that "Performance...must be substantially in compliance with the spirit and the letter of the contract", suggesting that a significant reduction in service levels could be a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: THIS MAINTENANCE AGREEMENT IS GOVERNED BY, AND SHALL BE SUBJECT TO, THE TERMS AND CONDITIONS OF THE MASTER AGREEMENT BETWEEN NETZEE AND BANKERS BANK, INCLUDING THE LIMITED WARRANTY AND DISCLAIMER AND LIMITATIONS OF LIABILITY PROVIDED THEREIN. SUCH TERMS AND CONDITIONS ARE HEREBY AFFIRMED BY BOTH PARTIES AND INCORPORATED BY REFERENCE IN THIS MAINTENANCE AGREEMENT, INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, ALL LICENSES GRANTED OR EXERCISABLE THEREUNDER.
perturbation.changed_text: THIS MAINTENANCE AGREEMENT IS GOVERNED BY THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF DELAWARE. ALL OTHER TERMS AND CONDITIONS ARE HEREBY DISCLAIMED.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement incorporates the terms of the Master Agreement, including warranty and liability limitations. Changing the governing law and disclaiming all other terms undermines the original agreement and could violate implied warranties under the UCC if the services are not fit for their intended purpose. Furthermore, changing the governing law might create legal disputes if the Master Agreement has a different choice of law.
perturbation.law_citation: UCC § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-314 implies a warranty of merchantability in contracts for the sale of goods, meaning the goods must be fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used. Disclaiming all terms and conditions, including the warranty provisions of the original Master Agreement, might violate the implied warranty of merchantability if the maintenance services are not performed adequately. The UCC applies as the maintenance includes software updates which are classified as a good. This is further compounded by switching the governing law to Delaware, when the contract was clearly intended to be performed with a Georgia banking corporation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly disclaims "all other terms and conditions," which directly contradicts UCC § 2-314's implied warranty of merchantability. The original agreement incorporated a Master Agreement with specific terms, including warranty provisions. By disclaiming all terms, the changed text attempts to remove these warranties, which is explicitly disallowed unless properly excluded or modified according to UCC § 2-316. The provided snippet states, "Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind." The changed text's disclaimer does not follow proper exclusion procedures, creating a contradiction.
